 INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — As of 2025-01-02
 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-01-02
 Events in window: 2 | Showing top 2
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[2025-01-02] Incoming West Virginia Gov. Patrick Morrisey Announces Initial Staff and Cabinet Appointments
West Virginia Governor-elect Patrick Morrisey announced his initial cabinet appointments and executive team members during a press conference in Charleston on January 2, 2025. Morrisey named ten individuals to his executive team, including Tom McCaffery as Chief of Staff, Douglas P. Buffington II as Senior Advisor, and Crescent Gallagher as Press Secretary. Other executive appointments included A. Todd Johnston as Deputy Chief of Staff and Sean M. Whelan as General Counsel. Five individuals were appointed to cabinet roles, such as Senator Eric Nelson as Secretary of Revenue, Eric Householder as Secretary of Administration, and Alex Mayer as Secretary of Human Services. Harold Ward was retained as Secretary of the Department of Environmental Protection, and Dianna Graves was named Commissioner of the Bureau of Senior Services. Cabinet appointments are subject to approval by the legislative body.
Key facts:
  • A. Todd Johnston (Deputy Chief of Staff and Chair of Energy, Infrastructure, and Competitiveness Council)
  • A. Todd Johnston (Deputy Chief of Staff, Chair of Energy, Infrastructure, and Competitiveness Council)
  • Alex Mayer (Secretary of Human Services)
  • Announcement made in Charleston, West Virginia
  • Cabinet appointments subject to legislative approval
  • Crescent Gallagher (Press Secretary)
  • Curtis R. A. Capehart (Director of Policy)
  • Dianna Graves (Commissioner of Bureau of Senior Services)
  • Douglas P. Buffington II (Senior Advisor)
  • Eric Householder (Secretary of Administration)
  • Harold Ward (Secretary of the Department of Environmental Protection)
  • John L. Findlay (Director of Intergovernmental and Political Affairs)
  • Katie Franklin (Deputy General Counsel)
  • Marissa Findlay (Director of Operations)
  • Mike McKown (Director of Budget)
  • Patrick Morrisey (Governor-elect)
  • Patrick Morrisey (Incoming West Virginia Governor)
  • Sean M. Whelan (General Counsel)
  • Sen. Eric Nelson (Secretary of Revenue)
  • Senator Eric Nelson (Secretary of Revenue)
  • The Honorable Dianna Graves (Commissioner of Bureau of Senior Services)
  • The Honorable Eric Householder (Secretary of Administration)
  • Tom McCaffery (Chief of Staff)
"I think he’s a home run get for the state of West Virginia. I can’t imagine having someone with that kind of experience. He’s going to help the citizens of our state tremendously." — Patrick Morrisey (News conference, referring to Tom McCaffery's appointment as Chief of Staff)
"I’m really enjoying the working relationship with Sen. Smith. I think he’s going to do a terrific job. I’ve certainly been talking with the speaker as well. I anticipate a good, close working relationship. We have a lot of work to do in the upcoming months. It’s a thrilling time in West Virginia. I’m ready to make sure that everyone knows our team is second to none. That’s how we did really well in the AG’s office." — Patrick Morrisey (News conference, discussing working relationship with legislative body for cabinet approvals)
"I am grateful to each of these leaders for their willingness and desire to bring opportunity and prosperity to the Mountain State. We are united in our mission of promoting and protecting the freedoms and rights of every West Virginian. When we work together, West Virginia wins." — Patrick Morrisey (Announcing cabinet and executive team appointments)
Sources: wchstv.com, www.wdtv.com (2 sources)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[2025-01-02] Analysts Project Within-Target Philippine Inflation for 2025, Paving Way for BSP Easing
Philippine inflation is projected to remain within the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas' (BSP) 2-4% target range in 2025. This is attributed to moderating global commodity prices and administrative measures, including tariff reductions on food items.

The ASEAN+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (AMRO) forecasts average inflation at 3.2% for 2025, consistent with its 2024 projection and a decrease from 6% in 2023. The BSP forecasts average inflation of 3.3% for 2025.

This outlook may allow the BSP to continue a monetary easing cycle, with some analysts forecasting rate cuts of 75-100 basis points in 2025. Potential upside risks to inflation include economic growth, minimum wage increases, supply-side disruptions from natural disasters, geopolitical tensions, peso depreciation, and the impact of U.S. trade policies.
Key facts:
  • 2023 average inflation: 6%
  • 2024 average inflation (January-November): 3.2%
  • 25-bp RRP rate cuts in August, October, and December 2024
  • AMRO's 2025 average inflation projection: 3.2%
  • Analysts' forecast for BSP rate cuts in 2025: 75-100 bps
  • BSP Governor: Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
  • BSP's 2-4% inflation target range
  • BSP's 2025 average inflation forecast: 3.3%
  • Current BSP policy rate: 5.75%
  • Donald J. Trump set to assume US presidency on January 20
  • Inflation breached target from April 2022 to November 2023
  • July 2024 inflation spike: 4.4%
  • Monetary Board reduced target reverse repurchase (RRP) rate by 75 basis points in 2024
  • Peso closed at P59 per dollar thrice in 2024
  • Philippine economy grew 5.2% in Q3 2024
  • US Federal Reserve began easing cycle in September 2024 with 50-bp cut
  • US Federal Reserve followed with two 25-bp reductions in November and December 2024
  • US Federal Reserve's target rate: 4.25%-4.5%
"Looking ahead, inflation will likely be contained reflecting the moderation in global commodity prices, and administrative measures such as tariff cuts on food items, particularly tariff cuts on imported rice from July." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing inflation outlook)
"Specifically, average inflation is projected to be 3.2% in 2025, the same level as in 2024, which is a substantial decline from the 6% in 2023." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing inflation projections)
"We still expect the Philippines’ inflation to remain within the BSP’s 2-4% target range." — Krisjanis Krustins (Director at Fitch Ratings’ Asia-Pacific Sovereigns team discussing Philippines' inflation outlook)
"In my new view, considering all risks and given past and current information, inflation in 2025 will still generally fall within the target range." — Enrico P. Villanueva (Senior lecturer at the University of the Philippines Los Baños Economics Department discussing 2025 inflation outlook)
"The inflation outlook for 2025 largely hinges on external factors like commodity prices and exchange rates, as well as domestic supply-side management." — John Paolo R. Rivera (Senior research fellow at the Philippine Institute for Development Studies discussing inflation drivers)
"While the BSP’s easing cycle is likely to continue, its trajectory will depend on inflation trends, peso stability, and global monetary policy movements." — John Paolo R. Rivera (Senior research fellow at the Philippine Institute for Development Studies discussing BSP policy trajectory)
"There is always a risk that a component of the consumer price index (CPI) will go up, like in electricity and wages as pointed out in BSP medium-term inflation path." — Enrico P. Villanueva (Senior lecturer at the University of the Philippines Los Baños Economics Department discussing inflation risks)
"There could be some upside risk to inflation due to robust economic growth and increases in minimum wages." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing inflation risks)
"Climate change impacts through potential strings of strong typhoons can make landfall and wreak havoc in Luzon and the Visayas." — Ruben Carlo O. Asuncion (Union Bank of the Philippines, Inc., Chief Economist discussing climate-related inflation risks)
"Meanwhile, worsening geopolitical tensions in other regions, such as Ukraine and the Middle East, could raise the risk of global supply disruption leading to sharp spikes in commodity prices and shipping costs, and cause another round of upward pressures on inflation." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing geopolitical inflation risks)
"The biggest risks to the inflation outlook stem from external developments, in particular trade policy, inflation and interest rates in the US, through their impact on the Philippine peso." — Krisjanis Krustins (Fitch Ratings director discussing external inflation risks)
"With inflation expected to remain within target, the BSP will also likely continue its rate-cutting cycle." — Ruben Carlo O. Asuncion (Union Bank Chief Economist discussing BSP easing)
"Over the past two years, a forceful monetary policy tightening during 2022 and 2023 that brought the policy rate to a 17-year high, together with the government’s direct measures, has helped bring headline inflation back to within the BSP’s inflation target of 2–4%." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing past BSP policy)
"From our point of view, there is room to gradually adjust the policy rate to a less restrictive stance in light of lower inflation." — Andrew Tsang (AMRO Senior Economist discussing future BSP policy)
"Given the calibrated behavior of the BSP in the past years, monetary policy in 2025 may continue to adopt a cautious approach in the first half of the year, closely monitoring both inflation and external conditions." — Krisjanis Krustins (Fitch Ratings director discussing BSP policy approach)
"delivering up to 100 bps worth of cuts in 2025 may be 'too much,' but noted that they are 'neither more dovish nor less dovish.'" — Eli M. Remolona, Jr. (BSP Governor discussing potential rate cuts)
"signaled the possibility of delivering a rate cut at the Monetary Board’s first policy meeting in 2025." — Eli M. Remolona, Jr. (BSP Governor discussing potential rate cuts)
"The main risk factor at the moment would be further strengthening of the dollar, for example due to the imposition of tariffs by the new US administration." — Krisjanis Krustins (Fitch Ratings director discussing risks to easing cycle)
"What can derail monetary easing is the thinking that all risks to inflation have to be eliminated (that is impossible), and that a high interest rate is the solution to price shocks, which usually affect supply rather than demand." — Enrico P. Villanueva (Senior lecturer at the University of the Philippines Los Baños Economics Department discussing risks to easing cycle)
Sources: www.bworldonline.com (1 source)

 END BRIEFING — 2 events retrieved
